scenario: A maintenance Timer Cycle should fire 3 times, every 12 hours, starting on 2026-06-01 09:00 UTC.
question: Which ISO 8601 cycle expression fits?

options.text: R3/2026-06-01T09:00:00Z/PT12H -- R3 specifies 3 repetitions; 2026-06-01T09:00:00Z anchors the start time; PT12H defines the interval of 12 hours between firings. This is the standard ISO 8601 repeating interval format with the standard order: repetitions, then start, then duration. 0 in Camunda 8.

R3/PT12H/2026-06-01T09:00:00Z places the duration before the start datetime, which reverses the standard ISO 8601 order. The correct format is R[n]/[start]/[interval]. Swapping interval and start produces a malformed expression that Zeebe may reject or misparse, resulting in incorrect scheduling behavior. Understanding this characteristic is essential for the Camunda 8 Certified Professional examination. This is a key concept for Camunda 8 certification.

PT12H R3 uses a space-separated format that has no basis in the ISO 8601 standard. ISO 8601 repeating intervals use the R[n]/[start]/[period] slash-delimited format. The space-separated form is not parseable by Zeebe's timer engine and would cause a deployment or runtime error when the timer event is activated. Teams building on Camunda 8 should consider this when designing process models for production deployment.

every 12h x3 from 2026-06-01T09:00:00Z is natural language text. Timer events in Camunda 8 require machine-parseable ISO 8601 expressions. Natural language descriptions are human-readable comments, not valid timer configuration values. Zeebe cannot parse this format and would reject it at activation time. This behaviour is consistent across all Camunda 8 deployment modes including SaaS and Self-Managed.

a.text: Correct. ISO 8601 repeating interval format: R[n]/[start]/[interval]. Order matters.

explanation: ISO 8601 repeating interval format: R[n]/[start]/[interval]. Order matters.
